course_info.title: Mobile and IoT Security
prerequisites: CY201 - Ethical Hacking and Penetration Testing

learning_objectives: Compare iOS and Android security architectures at the hardware and software layers
Understand application sandboxing, code signing, and permission models
Identify mobile-specific attack vectors and the evolving mobile threat landscape
Evaluate the security tradeoffs between closed and open mobile ecosystems
readings: OWASP Mobile Security Testing Guide Ch. 1-3
Android Security Architecture documentation
Apple Platform Security Guide - Hardware Security and Biometrics

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile devices have become the primary computing platform for over five billion users worldwide, carrying sensitive personal data, corporate credentials, banking information, and biometric identifiers. Unlike traditional desktop operating systems that evolved from multi-user time-sharing systems with relatively permissive inter-process communication, mobile operating systems were designed from the ground up with application isolation as a core architectural principle. iOS leverages a tightly controlled hardware-software integration with the Secure Enclave Processor, mandatory code signing, and a curated App Store review process that together create a defense-in-depth posture. Android takes a more open approach, using SELinux mandatory access controls, a capability-based permission model, and Google Play Protect scanning to balance openness with security. Both platforms implement application sandboxing through different mechanisms: iOS uses entitlements and the TrustCache, while Android enforces per-app Linux user IDs and SELinux contexts. Understanding these architectural differences is essential for security professionals who must assess mobile applications, design enterprise mobility policies, and evaluate the risk posture of devices accessing corporate resources. This session establishes the foundational knowledge of mobile platform security that will underpin all subsequent topics in the course.
key_concepts: iOS security architecture: Secure Enclave Processor for key management and biometrics, hardware-bound encryption keys, Data Protection API with four protection classes, mandatory code signing via Apple Developer certificates

Android security architecture: Linux kernel with SELinux mandatory access controls, application sandboxing via unique Linux UIDs, Verified Boot chain from hardware root of trust to OS, hardware-backed Keystore using TEE or StrongBox

Application sandboxing: each app runs in its own process with its own file system partition, IPC is restricted to declared interfaces, and apps cannot access other apps' private data without explicit permission grants

Permission models: iOS granular entitlements with runtime prompts for sensitive resources (camera, location, contacts), Android runtime permissions with normal vs dangerous classification and background access restrictions

Mobile threat landscape: malicious applications that bypass store review, network-based attacks exploiting insecure Wi-Fi connections, physical extraction attacks on lost or stolen devices, and supply chain attacks targeting SDKs and libraries

Code signing and app integrity: iOS requires all executable code to be signed by an Apple-issued certificate, preventing unsigned code execution; Android APK signing with v2/v3 schemes protecting against tampering
Biometric authentication security: Face ID and Touch ID operate within the Secure Enclave, biometric templates never leave the secure processor, and match decisions are cryptographically attested

Mobile OS update mechanisms: iOS simultaneous rollout to all supported devices, Android fragmented updates through OEM and carrier channels creating a patching gap that leaves millions of devices vulnerable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examining the iOS and Android security models side-by-side using an Android emulator with debug capabilities and iOS Simulator, demonstrating app sandboxing boundaries by attempting cross-app data access, inspecting SELinux contexts on Android, and showing how the permission model restricts camera and location access at runtime
setup_requirements: Android emulator with debug capabilities (Android Studio)
iOS Simulator (macOS required with Xcode)
Frida dynamic instrumentation toolkit installed
ADB (Android Debug Bridge) configured

review.value: Each app runs in its own process with its own file system partition. Apps cannot access other apps' data without explicit permission. This is the foundation of mobile security, fundamentally different from desktop environments where processes share user-level access.

iOS: closed ecosystem, mandatory code signing, App Store review, hardware-software integration. Android: open ecosystem, sideloading possible, Play Protect scanning, fragmented updates. iOS has smaller attack surface; Android has more flexibility but greater exposure to unvetted applications.

Both platforms isolate sensitive cryptographic operations in hardware-backed secure processors. iOS uses the Secure Enclave for Face ID, Touch ID, and Data Protection keys. Android uses the Trusted Execution Environment or StrongBox for Keystore operations.

iOS devices receive updates simultaneously from Apple. Android updates must pass through chipset vendors, OEMs, and carriers, creating months-long delays. This fragmentation means millions of Android devices remain vulnerable to patched CVEs for extended periods.

learning_objectives: Perform static and dynamic analysis of mobile applications
Identify OWASP Mobile Top 10 vulnerabilities in real applications
Use Frida for runtime manipulation and SSL pinning bypass
Evaluate insecure data storage and credential handling practices
readings: OWASP Mobile Top 10 2024
Frida documentation and hooking tutorials
OWASP Mobile Security Testing Guide Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile application security testing combines traditional application security methodology with mobile-specific techniques designed for analyzing apps running in sandboxed environments on constrained hardware. Unlike web application testing where a browser serves as the universal client, mobile apps are compiled binaries with platform-specific runtimes, encrypted communications, and hardware-backed credential storage. The testing methodology must address both the client-side application and its server-side API interactions. Static analysis involves decompiling the application binary to examine source code, configuration files, and embedded secrets without executing the app. Dynamic analysis instruments the running application to observe behavior, intercept network traffic, and manipulate runtime state. The OWASP Mobile Top 10 provides a structured framework for identifying the most critical mobile security risks, from improper credential usage and inadequate supply chain security to insecure authentication and insufficient cryptography. This session focuses on practical techniques for APK decompilation with jadx, iOS binary analysis, network traffic interception through SSL pinning bypass, and the discovery of insecure local data storage. Students will learn to build a repeatable mobile security testing methodology that covers the full attack surface of a mobile application.
key_concepts: OWASP Mobile Top 10: Improper Credential Usage, Inadequate Supply Chain Security, Insecure Authentication/Authorization, Insufficient Input/Output Validation, Insecure Communication, Inadequate Privacy Controls, Insufficient Binary Protections, Security Misconfiguration, Insecure Data Storage, Insufficient Cryptography

Static analysis: APK decompilation with jadx to recover Java/Kotlin source, IPA analysis with class-dump for Objective-C headers, searching for hardcoded API keys, secrets, and debug endpoints in decompiled code
Dynamic analysis: Frida hooking for runtime method interception and modification, tracing function calls, dumping decrypted data from memory, and bypassing client-side security controls
Network traffic interception: configuring Burp Suite as a proxy for mobile devices, installing custom CA certificates, and using Frida scripts to bypass SSL/TLS certificate pinning implementations
Insecure data storage: auditing SharedPreferences and SQLite databases on Android, inspecting NSUserDefaults and Keychain entries on iOS, identifying sensitive data stored without encryption
Binary protection assessment: checking for obfuscation, anti-debugging measures, root/jailbreak detection, and integrity verification mechanisms
API security testing: examining authentication token handling, session management, input validation on API endpoints, and authorization enforcement for privilege escalation
Third-party library vulnerabilities: identifying outdated SDKs with known CVEs, assessing advertising and analytics library data collection, and supply chain risk from transitive dependencies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Decompiling an Android APK with jadx to find hardcoded API keys and debug endpoints, then using Frida to bypass SSL pinning on the running application and intercepting API traffic through Burp Suite to discover broken authentication on the server-side API
setup_requirements: jadx decompiler for Android APK analysis
Frida with frida-server deployed on Android emulator
Burp Suite configured as mobile proxy with CA certificate
Test applications: InsecureBankv2, DIVA, OWASP MSTG apps

review.value: Apps pin the expected server certificate to prevent man-in-the-middle interception. Frida scripts hook the certificate validation function and force it to accept any certificate, enabling traffic interception for security testing. This is essential for dynamic analysis of API communications.

Storing sensitive data in SharedPreferences (Android) or UserDefaults (iOS) without encryption is a critical vulnerability. Root or jailbreak access exposes all app data. Remediation: use Android Keystore or iOS Keychain with biometric protection for sensitive values.

Mobile apps commonly include dozens of third-party SDKs for analytics, advertising, crash reporting, and social login. Each SDK is a potential supply chain risk: it may contain vulnerabilities, collect excessive data, or be compromised by attackers targeting the SDK vendor.

Decompiling mobile binaries frequently reveals hardcoded secrets, debug endpoints, and internal API documentation that developers assumed would be hidden in the compiled binary. Assume everything in the binary is recoverable by an attacker.

learning_objectives: Map the IoT attack surface across device, network, gateway, and cloud layers
Identify common IoT vulnerabilities using the OWASP IoT Top 10
Analyze IoT communication protocols for security weaknesses
Perform firmware extraction and initial analysis on IoT devices
readings: IoT Penetration Testing Cookbook Ch. 1-4
OWASP IoT Top 10 2024
NIST IR 8228 Considerations for Managing IoT Cybersecurity and Privacy Risks

lecture_notes.introduction: The Internet of Things extends the attack surface from traditional IT systems into the physical world, encompassing everything from industrial control systems and medical devices to consumer smart home products and connected vehicles. Unlike conventional IT assets that can be regularly patched, monitored, and replaced, IoT devices often operate with minimal computing resources, run firmware that is rarely updated, and are deployed in physically accessible locations for years or even decades. The IoT attack surface spans multiple layers: the physical device with its hardware interfaces and sensors, the local network with its wireless protocols, the gateway or edge device that bridges IoT networks to the internet, the cloud platform that processes and stores IoT data, and the management applications that control device fleets. Each layer presents distinct vulnerability categories. Device-level risks include weak default credentials, unprotected hardware debug interfaces, and insecure firmware update mechanisms. Network-level risks involve unencrypted communication protocols, lack of mutual authentication, and susceptibility to replay and relay attacks. Cloud-level risks mirror traditional web application vulnerabilities but with the added complexity of massive device fleets generating continuous telemetry data. This session provides a comprehensive framework for understanding and assessing IoT security across all layers.
key_concepts: IoT architecture layers: device (sensors, actuators, MCU), gateway (protocol translation, edge processing), network (wireless and wired transport), cloud (data processing, device management), application (user interfaces, dashboards)

OWASP IoT Top 10: weak/guessable/hardcoded passwords, insecure network services, insecure ecosystem interfaces, lack of secure update mechanism, use of insecure/outdated components, insufficient privacy protection, insecure data transfer/storage, lack of device management, insecure default settings, lack of physical hardening

IoT protocols: MQTT (pub/sub messaging), CoAP (constrained RESTful), BLE (short-range low-power), Zigbee (mesh networking), Z-Wave (home automation), LoRaWAN (long-range low-power), and their respective security capabilities and limitations

Firmware extraction and analysis: using binwalk to identify and extract filesystem images, searching for hardcoded credentials and cryptographic keys, identifying known vulnerable library versions through binary analysis

Physical attack vectors: JTAG and SWD debug interfaces for direct memory access, UART serial console access, SPI flash chip dumping for firmware extraction, and side-channel attacks on cryptographic implementations
Hardware security features: secure elements for key storage, physically unclonable functions for device identity, tamper-detection meshes, and fuse-based key provisioning
IoT vulnerability scanning: using tools like Shodan to discover exposed IoT devices, nmap service fingerprinting for IoT protocols, and specialized IoT vulnerability scanners
Supply chain concerns: counterfeit components, compromised firmware during manufacturing, and lack of software bill of materials for IoT device software stacks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Extracting firmware from a consumer IoT device via SPI flash chip reading, analyzing the firmware image with binwalk to extract the root filesystem, then searching for hardcoded credentials, default SSH keys, and known vulnerable library versions in the extracted filesystem
setup_requirements: IoT lab devices: smart plug, IP camera, or similar consumer IoT device
binwalk and firmware-mod-kit for firmware analysis
Bus Pirate or Flashrom-compatible SPI programmer for hardware interfaces
Ghidra or radare2 for binary analysis of extracted firmware components

review.value: MQTT is the most widely deployed IoT messaging protocol. Default configurations often lack authentication, allow wildcard topic subscriptions exposing all device data, and transmit telemetry unencrypted. Securing MQTT requires TLS transport, username/password or certificate authentication, and per-device topic ACLs.

binwalk identifies and extracts embedded filesystems from firmware images. Common findings: hardcoded credentials in configuration files, private keys for cloud API authentication, debug interfaces left enabled, and libraries with known CVEs that have not been updated in years.

IoT devices are deployed in physically accessible locations: homes, offices, public spaces. Any attacker with physical access can extract firmware, dump memory, attach debuggers, and extract cryptographic keys unless hardware security features specifically prevent these attacks.

IoT devices may be deployed for 10-20 years. The manufacturer may go out of business, stop releasing updates, or discontinue cloud services. Long-lived devices need update mechanisms that outlast their manufacturers, or organizations face mounting vulnerability risk.

class_schedule.topic: IoT Network Security and Protocol Analysis
learning_objectives: Analyze MQTT, CoAP, and BLE protocols for security vulnerabilities
Implement network-level protections for IoT deployments
Detect compromised IoT devices using network traffic analysis
Configure IoT network segmentation with dedicated VLANs and firewall policies
readings: IoT Security: Architecture and Solutions Ch. 5-7
BLE security research papers and Bluetooth SIG specifications
MQTT v5.0 specification - Security considerations

lecture_notes.introduction: IoT communication protocols were designed for constrained environments where bandwidth, power consumption, and processing capability are severely limited. This resource-constrained design philosophy often resulted in security being treated as an optional feature rather than a core requirement. MQTT, the dominant IoT messaging protocol, was originally designed without built-in authentication or encryption, relying on the transport layer for security. CoAP provides a lightweight RESTful interface for constrained devices but its UDP transport introduces challenges for stateful security. Bluetooth Low Energy enables short-range device communication with multiple pairing modes, several of which provide no protection against man-in-the-middle attacks. Understanding the security properties and limitations of each protocol is essential for designing secure IoT deployments. Beyond protocol-level security, network architecture plays a critical role in IoT defense. Proper segmentation isolates IoT devices from corporate networks, limiting the blast radius of a compromised device. Traffic analysis and behavioral monitoring can detect compromised IoT devices that exhibit anomalous communication patterns such as unexpected outbound connections, unusual data volumes, or communication with known malicious infrastructure. This session covers both protocol-level security analysis and network-level defensive architecture for IoT environments.
key_concepts: MQTT security: broker authentication via username/password or client certificates, TLS 1.2+ for transport encryption, Access Control Lists restricting per-client publish/subscribe topics, MQTT v5.0 enhanced authentication and authorization features

BLE security: four pairing modes (Just Works, Numeric Comparison, Passkey Entry, Out of Band) with varying MITM protection levels, Ubertooth-based BLE sniffing, and the vulnerability of legacy pairing to passive eavesdropping
CoAP security: DTLS for transport-layer security over UDP, block-wise transfer security implications, proxy security for CoAP-to-HTTP bridging, and resource discovery protection

IoT network segmentation: dedicated VLANs for IoT devices, firewall rules restricting IoT-to-corporate lateral movement, micro-segmentation policies per device type, and 802.1X port-based authentication for wired IoT devices

Traffic analysis for compromised IoT detection: establishing behavioral baselines per device type, detecting anomalous DNS queries, identifying unexpected outbound connections, monitoring data volume deviations

IoT gateway security: edge devices that bridge IoT protocols to IP networks, protocol translation security, local data filtering and anomaly detection at the gateway layer, and gateway hardening requirements
Wireless protocol attacks: Zigbee key extraction using killerbee, Z-Wave signal replay attacks, LoRaWAN session key recovery, and defense-in-depth approaches for wireless IoT networks
Network monitoring tools: Wireshark with IoT protocol dissectors, specialized IoT traffic analysis platforms, and MQTT debugging tools for protocol-level inspection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Intercepting MQTT traffic on an unencrypted broker using MQTT Explorer, subscribing to wildcard topics to observe all device data exposure, then configuring the Mosquitto broker with TLS certificates and per-device ACLs to demonstrate the security improvement
setup_requirements: Mosquitto MQTT broker with configurable security settings
MQTT Explorer client for protocol inspection
Ubertooth One for BLE sniffing demonstration (or pre-captured BLE traces)
Wireshark with IoT protocol dissectors installed

review.value: Bluetooth Low Energy 'Just Works' pairing mode provides no MITM protection and is the default for devices without displays or keyboards. An attacker with an Ubertooth can intercept pairing and decrypt all subsequent communication. Require 'Numeric Comparison' or 'Passkey Entry' modes for sensitive IoT devices.

IoT devices should be isolated on a dedicated VLAN with strict firewall rules: allow only required protocols to specific cloud endpoints, block all lateral communication to the corporate network, and monitor all IoT traffic flows for anomalies.

Subscribing to '#' on an unsecured MQTT broker returns messages from every topic, exposing all device telemetry, commands, and potentially credentials. ACLs must restrict each device to publish and subscribe only to its own topic namespace.

A smart thermostat should communicate only with its cloud endpoint on specific ports at predictable intervals. Sudden communication with unknown IP addresses, port scanning behavior, or data exfiltration volumes indicate compromise. Behavioral monitoring is more effective than signature-based detection for IoT.

class_schedule.topic: IoT Firmware Security and Secure Boot
learning_objectives: Implement a secure boot chain for IoT devices from ROM bootloader to OS
Design secure over-the-air firmware update mechanisms with rollback protection
Protect IoT devices against physical tampering and firmware extraction
Evaluate hardware security features including TPM, TrustZone, and secure elements
readings: Embedded Systems Security Ch. 6-8
ARM TrustZone documentation and security model
NIST SP 800-193 Platform Firmware Resiliency Guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: IoT devices operate in physically accessible environments where attackers can directly interact with hardware. Unlike servers locked in data centers, a smart lock on a door or a sensor in a parking structure is available for physical examination, firmware extraction, and hardware-level attacks. The security of an IoT device ultimately depends on its hardware root of trust and the integrity of the software chain that builds upon it. Secure boot ensures that each stage of the boot process verifies the integrity and authenticity of the next stage before transferring execution, creating an unbroken chain of trust from the immutable ROM bootloader to the running operating system and application code. Hardware security features such as ARM TrustZone, Trusted Platform Modules, and dedicated secure elements provide isolated execution environments for cryptographic operations and key storage that are resistant to software-based attacks. Secure firmware update mechanisms must ensure that only authentic, correctly signed firmware images are installed, that rollback to vulnerable versions is prevented, and that the update process itself does not create a window of vulnerability. This session covers the full spectrum of IoT firmware security, from hardware root of trust establishment through secure boot implementation to over-the-air update design, addressing both the theoretical foundations and practical implementation challenges that IoT security engineers face.
key_concepts: Secure boot chain: immutable ROM bootloader containing the root public key verifies the first-stage bootloader, which verifies the second-stage bootloader, which verifies the kernel, creating a chain of trust where each stage validates the next

Hardware root of trust: TPM for measured boot and key storage, ARM TrustZone for isolated secure world execution, dedicated secure elements (ATECC608A, SE050) for tamper-resistant cryptographic operations

Secure OTA firmware updates: signed firmware images using Ed25519 or ECDSA, encrypted transit via TLS, pre-installation signature verification against the embedded public key, anti-rollback protection using monotonic counters or fuse-based version tracking

Physical security measures: tamper-detection meshes that erase keys on case opening, epoxy encapsulation of debug interfaces, disabling JTAG/SWD via efuse after production, and secure element key storage resistant to side-channel attacks

Device lifecycle management: secure provisioning during manufacturing with unique per-device keys, operational monitoring for integrity violations, and secure decommissioning with key destruction and data erasure

A/B partition firmware scheme: maintaining two firmware partitions allows atomic updates with automatic rollback if the new firmware fails health checks, preventing devices from being bricked by failed updates
Code signing infrastructure: managing the firmware signing key hierarchy, offline root key storage in HSMs, intermediate signing keys for CI/CD pipeline integration, and key rotation procedures

Measured boot vs verified boot: verified boot blocks execution of tampered firmware; measured boot records the hash of each boot stage in the TPM and allows remote attestation to verify device integrity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a secure boot chain on a Raspberry Pi with verified firmware image signing using Ed25519, demonstrating that unsigned or tampered firmware images are rejected during boot, and showing how monotonic counter-based rollback protection prevents downgrade attacks
setup_requirements: Raspberry Pi with secure boot configuration capability
Firmware signing infrastructure with Ed25519 key pair
JTAG debugging tools for demonstrating physical attack vectors
U-Boot bootloader configured with verified boot

review.value: Each stage of the boot process verifies the digital signature of the next stage before executing it. The chain starts from an immutable ROM bootloader that trusts only a specific public key burned into hardware fuses. If any stage fails verification, boot halts.

Firmware updates must be signed by the manufacturer's private key, encrypted in transit via TLS, verified before installation, and support rollback protection using monotonic counters to prevent downgrade attacks to vulnerable versions.

Maintaining two firmware partitions allows the device to boot from the previous known-good partition if the new firmware fails. This prevents the catastrophic failure mode of a single-partition update that bricks the device if the update is corrupted or incompatible.

Each device must receive a unique cryptographic identity during manufacturing: a device certificate, private key stored in the secure element, and the manufacturer's root CA certificate. This identity enables mutual TLS authentication with cloud services.

class_schedule.topic: Mobile Device Management and BYOD Security
learning_objectives: Implement MDM policies for enterprise mobile deployments with enrollment workflows
Design BYOD security architectures using containerization and conditional access
Configure application whitelisting, VPN requirements, and compliance enforcement
Evaluate MDM platform capabilities and limitations for different organizational needs
readings: NIST SP 800-124 Rev. 2 Guidelines for Managing Mobile Devices
Microsoft Intune documentation and deployment guides
Apple Business Manager and DEP documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Enterprise mobile security extends beyond individual device protection to fleet-level management, policy enforcement, and continuous compliance monitoring across hundreds or thousands of devices. Mobile Device Management platforms provide centralized control over device configuration, application deployment, and security policy enforcement. The challenge intensifies with Bring Your Own Device programs, where organizations must protect corporate data on personally owned devices without overreaching into employees' private data. Modern MDM solutions address this through containerization, creating a managed workspace within the device that separates corporate apps and data from personal content. Conditional access policies gate access to corporate resources based on device compliance state: is the device encrypted, is the OS patched, is a screen lock configured, is the device rooted or jailbroken? This session covers the practical implementation of enterprise mobile security, from device enrollment workflows and policy configuration to application management and remote wipe capabilities. Students will learn to balance security requirements with user privacy and productivity, designing MDM deployments that protect organizational data while respecting employee expectations for personal device use.
key_concepts: MDM capabilities: automated device enrollment via DEP/Zero-Touch, policy push for encryption, passcode, and VPN requirements, application deployment and blacklisting, remote lock and selective/full wipe

BYOD security architectures: containerization with Android Work Profile or iOS managed apps, separating corporate data from personal data, user privacy preservation with limited management scope on personal devices

Conditional access policies: device must be compliant (encrypted, patched, non-rooted) before accessing corporate email, SharePoint, or other resources, with remediation guidance when compliance fails

Application security: managed app configuration pushing server URLs and certificates, app protection policies preventing copy-paste from corporate to personal apps, per-app VPN for corporate traffic isolation
Certificate-based authentication: deploying SCEP/ACME certificates to managed devices for Wi-Fi, VPN, and email authentication, automating certificate lifecycle management across the device fleet

Compliance monitoring: continuous assessment of device security posture, automated actions for non-compliant devices (restrict access, notify user, wipe after grace period), compliance reporting dashboards

Mobile threat defense integration: pairing MDM with MTD solutions that detect malicious apps, network attacks, and OS exploits on managed devices, feeding threat signals into conditional access decisions
Lost device procedures: graduated response from remote lock to selective wipe of corporate data to full device wipe, with clear policies defining when each action is appropriate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring MDM policies for an enterprise mobile fleet using Microsoft Intune: setting mandatory encryption requirements, deploying a VPN profile, configuring an application whitelist, and demonstrating how conditional access blocks a non-compliant device from accessing corporate email
setup_requirements: Microsoft Intune trial environment or similar MDM platform
Test mobile devices or emulators enrolled in MDM
Azure AD for conditional access policy configuration
Sample corporate applications for managed deployment

review.value: MDM enables remote data erasure on lost or stolen devices. Selective wipe removes only corporate data from the managed container. Full wipe factory-resets the entire device. For BYOD devices, selective wipe is appropriate; full wipe is reserved for corporate-owned devices.

Conditional access evaluates device compliance before granting access to corporate resources. A device that is unencrypted, running an outdated OS, or detected as jailbroken is blocked from corporate email and applications until it returns to compliance.

MDM on personal devices must limit management scope: no visibility into personal apps, browsing history, or location outside work hours. Containerization provides the technical boundary. Clear communication about what is and is not monitored builds employee trust.

SCEP-provisioned certificates eliminate password-based authentication for Wi-Fi and VPN. Certificates are tied to device identity, automatically renewed, and revoked when the device is unenrolled. This is more secure and provides better user experience than password rotation.

class_schedule.topic: IoT Fleet Security and Device Management
learning_objectives: Design security monitoring architectures for large IoT fleet deployments
Implement automated certificate rotation and key management for IoT fleets
Apply NIST guidelines for IoT device cybersecurity capability requirements
Build zero-trust architectures for IoT environments with microsegmentation
readings: NIST SP 800-183 Networks of Things
NIST IR 8259 IoT Device Cybersecurity Capability Core Baseline
AWS IoT Device Defender documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Securing individual IoT devices is necessary but insufficient for enterprise IoT deployments that may encompass thousands or tens of thousands of connected devices. Fleet-level security requires centralized device inventory, automated provisioning and decommissioning, continuous monitoring for anomalous behavior, and scalable key and certificate management. A manufacturing facility may deploy sensors on every production line, a hospital may connect thousands of medical devices to its network, and a smart building may integrate HVAC, lighting, access control, and fire safety systems into a unified IoT platform. Each deployment creates a fleet management challenge: how do you ensure that every device is running authorized firmware, that certificates are rotated before expiration across the entire fleet, that a compromised device is detected and isolated before it becomes a pivot point for lateral movement into corporate networks? The zero trust security model is particularly relevant for IoT environments where devices cannot run traditional endpoint security agents and where device identity must be verified for every network connection. This session addresses fleet-level IoT security management, covering NIST guidelines for IoT device capability requirements, automated certificate lifecycle management, behavioral monitoring at fleet scale, and zero-trust architecture patterns for IoT environments.
key_concepts: IoT fleet management: centralized device registry tracking firmware versions, certificate expiration dates, and security posture for every connected device, with automated alerting for non-compliant devices

Automated certificate rotation: EST (Enrollment over Secure Transport) and ACME protocols for fleet-scale certificate issuance and renewal without manual intervention, with fallback procedures for devices that fail to rotate

Zero trust for IoT: device identity verified via mutual TLS on every connection, microsegmentation limiting each device to only its required network paths, continuous device health monitoring informing access decisions

NIST IR 8259 capabilities: device identification, device configuration, data protection, logical access to interfaces, software update, and cybersecurity state awareness as baseline requirements for all IoT devices

Fleet behavioral monitoring: establishing per-device-type behavioral baselines for communication patterns, data volumes, and timing, then detecting deviations that indicate compromise using statistical and ML-based anomaly detection
Device provisioning at scale: zero-touch provisioning workflows that securely onboard new devices with unique certificates and configuration without manual intervention at each device

Regulatory compliance for IoT: GDPR implications for IoT data collection, medical device cybersecurity regulations (FDA guidance), and industry-specific IoT security standards (IEC 62443 for industrial IoT)
Cloud IoT platform security: AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, and Google Cloud IoT security features including device authentication, message encryption, and fleet-level security monitoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring a fleet monitoring dashboard that tracks firmware versions, certificate expiration dates, and behavioral anomalies across a simulated IoT fleet, then triggering an automated response when a device exhibits anomalous outbound communication patterns
setup_requirements: IoT device management platform (AWS IoT Core or similar)
Simulated IoT fleet with multiple device types
Certificate management infrastructure with EST server
Monitoring dashboard with anomaly detection capabilities

review.value: IoT devices use X.509 certificates for authentication. Certificates expire and must be rotated without manual intervention. EST and ACME protocols enable fleet-scale automatic rotation. Monitoring certificate expiration across thousands of devices requires centralized tracking and alerting.

Every IoT device connection must be authenticated (mutual TLS), authorized (device-specific access policies), and monitored (behavioral analysis). No device is trusted based on network location alone. Microsegmentation ensures a compromised device cannot reach beyond its required communications.

A fleet of identical smart meters should exhibit similar communication patterns. A single meter that suddenly begins port scanning, communicating with foreign IP addresses, or transmitting significantly more data than peers is likely compromised. Fleet-scale anomaly detection leverages peer comparison.

NIST IR 8259 defines six core cybersecurity capabilities every IoT device should support: device identification, device configuration, data protection, logical access control, software update capability, and cybersecurity state awareness. Use this as a procurement checklist.

learning_objectives: Reverse engineer Android and iOS application binaries to understand internal logic
Bypass anti-tampering, root detection, and obfuscation protections
Extract cryptographic keys and algorithm implementations from compiled code
Assess the effectiveness of binary protection mechanisms
readings: OWASP Mobile Security Testing Guide - Reverse Engineering chapters
Android Reverse Engineering with Ghidra tutorials
iOS App Reverse Engineering by Snoolie K

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile application reverse engineering is the process of analyzing compiled application binaries to understand their internal logic, extract embedded secrets, and bypass client-side security controls. Security professionals must understand reverse engineering both offensively, to identify vulnerabilities in applications they are assessing, and defensively, to evaluate the effectiveness of binary protection mechanisms their organizations deploy. Android applications are distributed as APK files containing Dalvik bytecode that decompiles readily back to near-source-quality Java or Kotlin using tools like jadx and apktool. iOS applications are compiled to native ARM machine code that requires more sophisticated analysis with disassemblers like Ghidra or Hopper, though Swift and Objective-C metadata often provides rich type information. Developers attempt to protect their applications through obfuscation, anti-debugging checks, root and jailbreak detection, integrity verification, and certificate pinning. Each of these protections can be bypassed by a determined analyst, but the combination raises the effort required and deters casual attackers. This session covers practical techniques for both Android and iOS reverse engineering, including decompilation, disassembly, dynamic instrumentation with Frida, and the systematic bypassing of common protection mechanisms.
key_concepts: Android decompilation: APK structure (DEX, resources, manifest), jadx for Java/Kotlin source recovery, apktool for resource and manifest extraction, smali-level patching for behavioral modification
iOS binary analysis: Mach-O binary format, class-dump for Objective-C headers, Ghidra for ARM64 disassembly, Swift metadata recovery for type information and function signatures

Frida dynamic instrumentation: method hooking to intercept and modify function arguments and return values at runtime, JavaScript injection for runtime exploration, Frida Gadget for non-rooted device testing

Root and jailbreak detection bypass: common detection methods (file existence checks, binary execution tests, Cydia detection) and systematic bypass using Frida hooks to return false for all detection checks

Anti-debugging and integrity checks: ptrace-based anti-debugging, checksum verification of the application binary, SafetyNet/Play Integrity API attestation, and techniques for bypassing each protection

Obfuscation assessment: ProGuard/R8 name obfuscation on Android, string encryption, control flow obfuscation, native code migration, and the limits of each technique against dedicated reverse engineering

Cryptographic implementation analysis: extracting encryption keys from application memory using Frida, identifying weak cryptographic choices (ECB mode, hardcoded IVs, custom algorithms), and assessing key derivation functions
Automated analysis: using MobSF (Mobile Security Framework) for automated static and dynamic analysis of mobile application binaries, integrating results into security assessment workflows
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reverse engineering an Android application that implements root detection, SSL pinning, and encrypted local storage: first decompiling with jadx to understand the protection logic, then using Frida scripts to bypass root detection and SSL pinning, and finally extracting the encryption key from runtime memory to decrypt the local database
setup_requirements: jadx and apktool for Android static analysis
Ghidra configured with ARM processor support for iOS analysis
Frida toolkit with scripts for common bypass techniques
MobSF (Mobile Security Framework) for automated analysis

review.value: Android APKs decompile to near-source-quality code even with ProGuard obfuscation, which only renames identifiers. Assume every logic path, algorithm, and hardcoded value in a mobile application will be recovered by a motivated attacker. Never rely on client-side secrecy.

No single protection mechanism (obfuscation, root detection, anti-debugging) is sufficient alone. Effective binary protection layers multiple mechanisms: the combination increases the time and skill required for bypass, even though each individual protection is bypassable.

Even when keys are encrypted or derived at runtime, Frida can intercept the decrypted key in memory at the moment it is used. The only defense is hardware-backed key storage (Keystore/Secure Enclave) where the key never enters application memory.

Client-side security controls (root detection, integrity checks, certificate pinning) are deterrents, not guarantees. All security-critical decisions must be enforced on the server. The client should be treated as untrusted regardless of what protections are in place.

learning_objectives: Analyze Bluetooth Classic, BLE, and NFC security mechanisms and known vulnerabilities
Perform wireless protocol security assessments using specialized tools
Evaluate relay and replay attack risks for contactless systems
Design secure wireless communication architectures for mobile and IoT applications
readings: Bluetooth SIG Security Specifications v5.3
NFC Forum security specifications
Research papers on BLURtooth, KNOB, and BIAS Bluetooth attacks

lecture_notes.introduction: Short-range wireless protocols including Bluetooth Classic, Bluetooth Low Energy, and Near Field Communication form the communication backbone for mobile device peripherals, IoT sensors, contactless payment systems, and access control solutions. Each protocol was designed with specific power, range, and throughput constraints that influenced their security architectures. Bluetooth Classic implements a full security manager with link-layer encryption and pairing mechanisms but has suffered from multiple protocol-level vulnerabilities including KNOB (Key Negotiation of Bluetooth), BIAS (Bluetooth Impersonation Attacks), and BLURtooth. BLE simplified the security model for low-power devices, introducing four pairing modes with vastly different security guarantees. NFC operates at extremely short range but enables relay attacks where an attacker bridges the NFC communication over a longer distance. Contactless payment systems and access control badges built on NFC must defend against cloning, eavesdropping, and relay attacks. Understanding the security properties of these wireless protocols is essential for assessing mobile payment applications, IoT device communications, physical access control systems, and healthcare device interfaces.
key_concepts: Bluetooth Classic security: Secure Simple Pairing modes, link-layer encryption with E0 stream cipher, known vulnerabilities (KNOB attack forcing 1-byte entropy, BIAS impersonation, BLURtooth cross-transport key derivation)

BLE security levels: Mode 1 (no security, Just Works, unauthenticated pairing, authenticated LE Secure Connections), MITM vulnerability in Just Works and legacy pairing, BLE 4.2+ LE Secure Connections with ECDH key exchange

NFC security: short-range operation (typically under 10cm), relay attack using two NFC-enabled devices to bridge communication over distance, eavesdropping with directional antennas, data manipulation during transmission
Contactless payment security: EMV contactless protocol, tokenization replacing real card numbers, transaction limits, and the distinction between card-present and card-not-present fraud prevention
Access control badge cloning: Proxmark3 for reading and emulating RFID/NFC badges, upgrading from legacy HID protocols to modern encrypted badge systems, multi-factor physical access

Bluetooth reconnaissance: scanning for discoverable devices, fingerprinting device types and services, identifying vulnerable firmware versions, and mapping the Bluetooth attack surface in a target environment

Secure wireless design patterns: authenticated pairing with user confirmation, encrypted transport for all data exchange, mutual authentication between devices, session key rotation, and out-of-band channel verification

Regulatory and health considerations: Bluetooth and NFC power limits, electromagnetic compatibility requirements, and the interaction between wireless security protocols and medical device regulations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing BLE reconnaissance and sniffing using a BLE-capable adapter, analyzing pairing exchanges for security weaknesses, then demonstrating an NFC relay attack scenario where a contactless card transaction is bridged over a network connection to a remote terminal
setup_requirements: BLE-capable USB adapter with monitor mode support
Bluetooth analysis tools (btlejack, BLE sniffer)
NFC reader/writer hardware (ACR122U or similar)
Proxmark3 for RFID/NFC assessment (optional)

review.value: Key Negotiation of Bluetooth attack forces Bluetooth Classic devices to negotiate a 1-byte encryption key, which can be brute-forced in real time. Affects all Bluetooth devices prior to the specification fix. Demonstrates the importance of minimum key length enforcement.

An attacker places one device near the victim's contactless card and another near a payment terminal, relaying the NFC protocol over a network connection. The terminal sees a valid card transaction. Defense: distance bounding protocols, requiring PIN for transactions, time-based relay detection.

Just Works pairing provides no MITM protection and should never be used for sensitive IoT devices. LE Secure Connections with Numeric Comparison provides ECDH key exchange with visual confirmation, the strongest BLE pairing mode currently available.

Legacy 125kHz RFID badges (HID ProxCard) transmit credentials in the clear and are trivially cloned. Upgrading to 13.56MHz encrypted protocols (DESFire EV2/EV3, SEOS) with mutual authentication prevents cloning. Multi-factor physical access adds PIN or biometric verification.

class_schedule.topic: Mobile Payment and Digital Wallet Security
learning_objectives: Analyze the security architecture of Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Samsung Pay
Evaluate tokenization, device-bound credentials, and transaction authorization mechanisms
Assess mobile banking application security against industry-specific threats
Design secure mobile payment integrations for e-commerce applications
readings: Apple Pay Security Overview - Apple Platform Security Guide
EMVCo Payment Tokenisation Specification
PCI Mobile Payment Acceptance Security Guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile payment systems have transformed how consumers transact, processing trillions of dollars annually through Apple Pay, Google Pay, Samsung Pay, and banking applications. The security architecture of these systems is remarkably sophisticated, combining hardware security modules, tokenization, biometric authentication, and cryptographic transaction authorization into a defense-in-depth payment processing pipeline. When a user adds a credit card to Apple Pay, the actual card number is never stored on the device or transmitted during transactions. Instead, a device-specific token called a Device Account Number is provisioned by the payment network and stored in the Secure Enclave. Each transaction generates a unique dynamic security code using the device-bound key, making intercepted transaction data useless for fraud. Google Pay follows a similar architecture using Host Card Emulation with cloud-based tokenization. Mobile banking applications face a different threat model: they must protect authentication credentials, transaction integrity, and account data against a wide range of attacks from keyloggers and screen capture malware to overlay attacks and accessibility service abuse.
key_concepts: Apple Pay architecture: Device Account Number stored in Secure Enclave, transaction-specific dynamic security codes generated by the secure element, biometric authentication (Face ID/Touch ID) required for each payment, never transmitting the actual card number

Google Pay HCE: Host Card Emulation using cloud-based tokenization, Android Keystore for credential protection, device-level risk assessment via Play Integrity API, limited-use virtual card numbers for in-store transactions

EMV tokenization: payment networks (Visa, Mastercard) issue device-specific tokens replacing real PANs, token lifecycle management including provisioning, suspension, and revocation, domain restrictions limiting token use to specific devices and merchant types

Mobile banking threats: overlay attacks (malicious apps placing invisible layers over banking UI), accessibility service abuse for automated credential theft, screen capture and keylogging by malware, SIM swap attacks for SMS OTP interception

Transaction authorization security: step-up authentication for high-risk transactions, device binding preventing credential transfer, geolocation verification, and behavioral biometrics detecting account takeover

PCI compliance for mobile: PCI DSS and PCI Mobile Payment Acceptance requirements, scope reduction through tokenization and point-to-point encryption, compliance implications of mobile SDK integration
QR code payment security: risks of QR code replacement attacks, URL injection, and merchant impersonation in QR-based payment systems used widely in markets outside the US
Fraud detection and prevention: real-time transaction risk scoring using device signals, behavioral patterns, and transaction context, with automated blocking of anomalous transactions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the Apple Pay transaction flow using packet capture and NFC protocol analysis to observe tokenization in action, then examining a mobile banking application for overlay attack vulnerability and demonstrating how accessibility service permissions could be abused for credential theft
setup_requirements: NFC protocol analyzer for contactless payment analysis
Mobile banking test application with known vulnerabilities
Android device with developer options for accessibility service testing
Frida scripts for payment application runtime analysis

review.value: Tokenization replaces the actual card number with a device-specific token that is useless if intercepted. Even if an attacker captures the full NFC transaction, the token cannot be used on a different device or for card-not-present fraud. This is the foundation of mobile payment security.

Malicious apps request the SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW permission to draw invisible overlays on top of banking apps, capturing credentials entered by the user. Defense: banking apps should detect overlay windows, use FLAG_SECURE to prevent screen capture, and implement client certificate authentication.

SMS OTP for banking authentication is vulnerable to SIM swap attacks where attackers convince carriers to transfer the victim's phone number. Banks are migrating to push-notification-based authentication, hardware tokens, and FIDO2 authenticators that are resistant to SIM swap.

Payment credentials are bound to specific device hardware through the secure element or hardware-backed keystore. This ensures that even if credentials are extracted through a software exploit, they cannot be used on a different physical device.

learning_objectives: Assess security risks specific to industrial control systems and SCADA environments
Apply the Purdue Model for ICS network segmentation and defense architecture
Analyze ICS-specific protocols (Modbus, DNP3, OPC UA) for security vulnerabilities
Design defense-in-depth strategies for operational technology environments
readings: NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3 Guide to OT Security
IEC 62443 Industrial Automation Security Standards overview
CISA ICS-CERT advisories and best practices

lecture_notes.introduction: Industrial Internet of Things and Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition systems manage critical infrastructure including power grids, water treatment facilities, manufacturing plants, and transportation systems. Unlike consumer IoT where a security failure might compromise personal data, ICS/SCADA security failures can cause physical damage, environmental disasters, and loss of human life. The convergence of operational technology with information technology has exposed previously air-gapped industrial systems to network-based attacks. Industrial protocols like Modbus, DNP3, and BACnet were designed decades ago for serial communication in trusted environments and lack basic security features such as authentication and encryption. The Purdue Reference Model provides a hierarchical framework for segmenting industrial networks into zones with controlled data flows between enterprise IT and operational technology. Attacks on industrial systems including Stuxnet, Triton/TRISIS, and the Colonial Pipeline incident demonstrate that nation-states and criminal organizations actively target industrial infrastructure. This session addresses the unique security challenges of industrial IoT environments.
key_concepts: Purdue Model: Level 0 (physical process), Level 1 (basic control), Level 2 (area supervisory), Level 3 (site operations), Level 3.5 (DMZ), Level 4 (enterprise IT), Level 5 (internet), with firewalls and data diodes controlling traffic flow between levels

ICS-specific protocols: Modbus TCP/RTU (no authentication, no encryption, trivially spoofable), DNP3 (optional Secure Authentication in v5), OPC UA (built-in security with certificates and encryption), BACnet (building automation with minimal security)

IEC 62443 security levels: SL1 (casual/coincidental), SL2 (intentional, low resources), SL3 (sophisticated, moderate resources), SL4 (state-sponsored, extensive resources), applied to zones and conduits in the industrial network

IT/OT convergence risks: connecting previously isolated industrial networks to enterprise IT for monitoring and analytics, remote access requirements, and cloud-based SCADA introducing new attack paths

Safety Instrumented Systems: SIS are the last line of defense preventing physical harm; Triton malware specifically targeted SIS controllers to disable safety mechanisms while manipulating the physical process

Industrial vulnerability management: challenges of patching real-time control systems with 24/7 uptime requirements, vendor-dependent patching cycles, and compensating controls when patching is not feasible

Network monitoring for ICS: deep packet inspection of industrial protocols, detecting unauthorized commands to PLCs, monitoring for configuration changes, and deploying passive monitoring that does not interfere with process control

Incident response in OT: prioritizing safety and availability over forensic preservation, safe shutdown procedures, coordination with plant operators, and regulatory reporting requirements for critical infrastructure incidents
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a simulated Modbus TCP environment with a PLC simulator and HMI, demonstrating how an unauthenticated attacker can read process variables and write coil values to manipulate the simulated industrial process, then implementing network monitoring to detect these unauthorized commands
setup_requirements: Modbus TCP simulator (OpenPLC or ModRSsim2)
Modbus client tools for protocol interaction
Wireshark with industrial protocol dissectors
Network monitoring with ICS-specific detection rules

review.value: Modbus TCP has no authentication, no encryption, and no integrity checking. Any device on the network can read all registers and write any coil value, potentially shutting down or damaging industrial equipment. Segmentation and monitoring are the primary defenses since replacing the protocol is often infeasible.

The Purdue Model creates defense-in-depth for industrial networks. The IT/OT DMZ at Level 3.5 is critical: no direct traffic flows from Level 4 (enterprise) to Level 2 (control) or below. All data exchange passes through jump servers, historians, and data diodes in the DMZ.

Safety Instrumented Systems must be on a physically separate network from the basic control system. The Triton attack demonstrated that compromising SIS while manipulating the process could cause physical destruction. SIS network isolation is a non-negotiable security requirement.

Production control systems often cannot be taken offline for patching. Compensating controls include virtual patching with ICS-aware IPS, network segmentation, application whitelisting on HMI/engineering workstations, and reducing attack surface by disabling unnecessary services.

class_schedule.topic: Connected Vehicle and Automotive IoT Security
learning_objectives: Analyze the attack surface of connected vehicles across CAN bus, infotainment, and V2X interfaces
Assess Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, cellular, and OBD-II interfaces for remote exploitation risks
Evaluate automotive security standards including ISO/SAE 21434 and UNECE WP.29
Design security architectures for vehicle-to-everything communication systems
readings: ISO/SAE 21434 Road Vehicles Cybersecurity Engineering overview
UNECE WP.29 Regulation on Cybersecurity and Software Updates
Miller and Valasek Jeep Cherokee remote exploitation research paper

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern connected vehicles contain over 100 electronic control units communicating over internal networks including CAN bus, LIN, FlexRay, and automotive Ethernet. These vehicles also present multiple external attack surfaces through Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, cellular connections, USB ports, OBD-II diagnostic interfaces, and emerging Vehicle-to-Everything communication systems. The 2015 Miller and Valasek demonstration of remote Jeep Cherokee exploitation through the cellular-connected infotainment system, ultimately reaching the CAN bus to control steering and braking, revealed the catastrophic potential of connected vehicle vulnerabilities. Automotive cybersecurity has since matured rapidly, with ISO/SAE 21434 establishing a comprehensive engineering standard for cybersecurity risk management across the vehicle lifecycle and UNECE WP.29 mandating cybersecurity management systems for vehicle type approval. The CAN bus protocol, designed in the 1980s for reliability in noisy environments, provides no authentication, encryption, or access control, meaning any node on the bus can send any message including safety-critical commands.
key_concepts: CAN bus architecture: broadcast protocol with no authentication or encryption, arbitration-based priority, any node can transmit any message ID, and injection attacks that can control safety-critical functions like braking, steering, and acceleration
Vehicle attack surface mapping: remote (cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, TPMS), short-range (NFC key fobs, V2X), local (OBD-II, USB, CD/DVD), and physical (ECU extraction, debug interfaces)

Automotive Ethernet and domain architecture: transitioning from flat CAN bus to domain-based architecture with gateway ECUs that filter and validate messages between domains (powertrain, chassis, infotainment, ADAS)

V2X security: Vehicle-to-Vehicle and Vehicle-to-Infrastructure communication using IEEE 1609.2 security with PKI-based certificate management, pseudonymous certificates for privacy, and misbehavior detection

ISO/SAE 21434: threat analysis and risk assessment (TARA), cybersecurity goals, cybersecurity claims, continuous monitoring, and incident response across the vehicle lifecycle from concept through decommissioning

UNECE WP.29: regulatory requirement for Cybersecurity Management System (CSMS) and Software Update Management System (SUMS) for vehicle type approval, applicable in EU, Japan, South Korea, and other UNECE member states

Key fob relay attacks: amplifying the wireless signal from a key fob inside a house to unlock and start a car in the driveway, and defenses including motion-sensing fobs, UWB distance bounding, and PIN-to-drive

Automotive software update security: signed firmware updates for ECUs, secure boot chains for safety-critical controllers, FOTA (firmware over the air) update infrastructure security, and rollback protection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interacting with a CAN bus simulator to demonstrate message injection and spoofing attacks, showing how an attacker on the CAN bus can forge instrument cluster readings and inject diagnostic commands, then implementing a CAN bus intrusion detection system that identifies anomalous message patterns
setup_requirements: CAN bus simulator with virtual ECUs
CAN interface adapter (CANable or similar)
can-utils Linux tools for CAN bus interaction
Wireshark with CAN protocol dissector

review.value: CAN bus is a broadcast medium where every ECU receives every message. There is no sender authentication: if an attacker connects to the CAN bus (via OBD-II or a compromised ECU), they can send messages that control any vehicle function. Gateway ECUs and message authentication are the primary defenses.

The Miller/Valasek attack chain: exploit the cellular-connected infotainment system remotely, pivot through the internal network to the CAN bus gateway, bypass gateway filtering, and inject CAN messages to control physical vehicle functions. Defense requires segmentation, gateway hardening, and CAN message authentication.

Relay attacks amplify the key fob signal over a long distance, tricking the car into thinking the fob is nearby. UWB-based proximity measurement detects relay by measuring signal round-trip time, providing distance bounding that relay cannot defeat without introducing measurable delay.

Vehicles remain on the road for 15-20 years. The cybersecurity management system must support software updates, vulnerability monitoring, and incident response for the entire vehicle lifecycle, far longer than typical IT equipment. UNECE WP.29 mandates this capability.

learning_objectives: Assess the security architecture of major IoT cloud platforms (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub)
Implement secure device-to-cloud communication with mutual TLS and message-level encryption
Design data pipeline security for IoT telemetry processing and storage
Evaluate serverless IoT architectures for security benefits and risks
readings: AWS IoT Core Security Best Practices
Azure IoT Hub Security documentation
Cloud Security Alliance IoT Security Controls Framework

lecture_notes.introduction: IoT cloud platforms serve as the central nervous system for modern IoT deployments, handling device authentication, message routing, data processing, device management, and fleet-level security monitoring. Major cloud providers have developed specialized IoT services that address the unique challenges of managing millions of constrained devices that communicate intermittently over unreliable networks. AWS IoT Core provides a scalable MQTT broker with X.509 certificate-based mutual authentication, fine-grained authorization policies, and integration with the broader AWS security ecosystem. Azure IoT Hub offers device provisioning services, twin-based configuration management, and integration with Azure Defender for IoT. These platforms shift many security responsibilities from the device to the cloud, but they also introduce new attack surfaces: compromised cloud credentials can affect entire device fleets, misconfigured IoT policies can grant excessive permissions, and insecure data pipelines can expose sensitive telemetry to unauthorized access. The security of the cloud platform is as critical as the security of the devices themselves, because the cloud layer can be leveraged to push malicious firmware, send unauthorized commands, or exfiltrate aggregated device data at scale.
key_concepts: Device authentication: X.509 certificate-based mutual TLS for device-to-cloud connections, certificate provisioning via fleet provisioning templates, Just-in-Time provisioning for manufacturing integration, custom authorizers for legacy device authentication

Authorization policies: AWS IoT policies controlling per-device publish/subscribe topic access, Azure IoT Hub shared access policies and per-device credentials, principle of least privilege for device permissions

Device shadow/twin: cloud-side representation of device desired and reported state, security implications of shadow access (reading shadows reveals device configuration, writing shadows can modify device behavior)

Message security: TLS transport encryption for MQTT connections, message-level payload encryption for sensitive data that must remain encrypted through the processing pipeline, message integrity verification

Data pipeline security: securing IoT data as it flows through message routing, stream processing, and storage services, encryption at rest for all telemetry databases, access control for analytics queries on device data

Fleet-level operations security: firmware update distribution through IoT jobs, remote command execution on device groups, and the catastrophic impact of a compromised cloud account on the entire fleet
IoT-specific misconfigurations: overly permissive IoT policies using wildcards, unrotated device certificates, disabled audit logging, public MQTT endpoints, and default shared access keys

Edge computing security: IoT edge runtimes (AWS Greengrass, Azure IoT Edge) executing workloads on gateway devices, container isolation for edge modules, and the expanded attack surface of edge computing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring an AWS IoT Core deployment with per-device X.509 certificates, fine-grained topic-based authorization policies, and audit logging, then demonstrating how an overly permissive wildcard policy allows a compromised device to subscribe to all fleet topics and send commands to other devices
setup_requirements: AWS IoT Core or Azure IoT Hub trial account
IoT device simulators using MQTT client libraries
Certificate generation and management tools
CloudTrail or Azure Monitor for audit log review

review.value: Both the device and the cloud endpoint present certificates and verify each other's identity. This prevents unauthorized devices from connecting to the platform and ensures devices connect only to the legitimate cloud endpoint, not a man-in-the-middle.

An IoT policy allowing publish and subscribe on 'topic/*' grants access to every device's topic. A compromised device can then eavesdrop on all fleet telemetry and send commands to other devices. Policies must restrict each device to its own topic namespace.

Device shadows store desired and reported state in the cloud. A compromised cloud account with shadow write access can modify the desired state of every device in the fleet, potentially pushing dangerous configurations or triggering harmful actions at scale.

Edge computing moves processing closer to devices, reducing latency and cloud dependency. But edge devices are physically accessible and may lack the security controls of cloud data centers. Container isolation, encrypted storage, and secure boot on edge gateways are essential.

learning_objectives: Develop incident response procedures specific to mobile device and IoT compromises
Perform mobile device forensic acquisition and analysis using accepted methodologies
Handle IoT device compromise with containment strategies that preserve operational safety
Coordinate multi-stakeholder response involving device manufacturers, cloud providers, and regulators
readings: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 Computer Security Incident Handling Guide
Mobile Device Forensics - SANS DFIR poster
ICS-CERT incident response procedures for IoT and OT environments

lecture_notes.introduction: Incident response for mobile and IoT environments presents unique challenges that differ substantially from traditional IT incident handling. Mobile device forensics must contend with full-disk encryption, secure enclaves that resist extraction, and the legal complexities of BYOD devices where corporate and personal data are intermingled. IoT incident response must balance security containment with operational safety, especially in industrial and medical contexts where shutting down a compromised device may cause greater harm than the compromise itself. The ephemeral nature of IoT device memory means that forensic evidence may be lost if the device is powered off or reset without proper acquisition. Mobile devices generate rich forensic artifacts including application data, communication logs, location history, and network connection records, but accessing this data often requires specialized tools and may be legally restricted. IoT devices typically offer limited forensic capability, requiring network traffic analysis and cloud-side log review as primary evidence sources. This session covers the full incident response lifecycle for mobile and IoT compromises, from initial detection and triage through containment, eradication, recovery, and lessons learned.
key_concepts: Mobile device forensic acquisition: logical acquisition (file-level), full filesystem acquisition (root/jailbreak required), physical acquisition (chip-off for damaged devices), and cloud acquisition from synced services

Mobile forensic tools: Cellebrite UFED for comprehensive extraction, Magnet AXIOM for cross-platform analysis, open-source tools (libimobiledevice, Android backup extraction), and Frida for runtime data extraction from running apps

BYOD incident legal considerations: establishing authority to examine personal devices, separating corporate from personal data during examination, chain of custody for potential legal proceedings, and employee privacy protections

IoT containment strategies: network isolation of compromised devices without disrupting the broader IoT deployment, firmware rollback to known-good versions, credential rotation for affected device certificates, and monitoring for lateral movement to other devices

IoT forensic limitations: constrained devices lack persistent storage for detailed logs, volatile memory is lost on power cycling, and firmware extraction may be the primary forensic artifact available for analysis
Network-based evidence: capturing and analyzing IoT network traffic for command-and-control communication, data exfiltration indicators, lateral scanning activity, and protocol anomalies
Cloud-side investigation: reviewing IoT platform audit logs for unauthorized device operations, API access anomalies, firmware distribution tampering, and device shadow/twin modification history

Coordinated response: engaging device manufacturers for vulnerability information and firmware updates, cloud platform security teams for infrastructure investigation, and regulatory notification when compromised IoT devices affect critical infrastructure or personal data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing a forensic analysis workflow for a compromised mobile device: acquiring data using logical extraction, analyzing application artifacts, extracting communication records, and correlating device evidence with cloud-side IoT platform logs to reconstruct the full timeline of a simulated breach scenario
setup_requirements: Mobile forensic acquisition tools (Cellebrite or open-source alternatives)
Android device with test data for forensic extraction
IoT platform audit logs for cloud-side investigation
Network packet captures from IoT environment for traffic analysis

review.value: Modern mobile devices use hardware-backed full-disk encryption that cannot be bypassed without the user's passcode. Secure Enclave rate-limiting prevents brute force. Forensic acquisition of locked devices increasingly requires legal compulsion of the device owner or exploitation of unpatched vulnerabilities.

In industrial and medical IoT, blindly isolating a compromised device may cause greater harm than the compromise. A compromised ventilator controller must be handled differently than a compromised smart thermostat. Incident response plans must account for safety implications of containment actions.

For IoT devices with limited forensic capability, network traffic captures often provide the most valuable evidence. Ensuring that network monitoring captures full packet data (not just metadata) for IoT VLANs is critical for post-incident investigation.

Mobile and IoT evidence is volatile: devices auto-update, sync to cloud, or power cycle. Immediate actions upon detection: enable airplane mode on mobile devices (preserve location data, prevent remote wipe), capture IoT network traffic, and preserve cloud-side logs before retention periods expire.

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Threats and Future of Mobile/IoT Security
learning_objectives: Evaluate emerging security challenges from 5G, edge computing, and AI-powered IoT systems
Assess post-quantum cryptography implications for IoT device security
Analyze security and privacy risks of ambient computing and smart environment deployments
Design forward-looking security strategies that anticipate evolving mobile and IoT threat landscapes
readings: 5G Security Architecture Specifications (3GPP TS 33.501)
NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography Standardization Status
IEEE reports on edge computing security challenges

lecture_notes.introduction: The mobile and IoT landscape is undergoing rapid transformation driven by 5G networks enabling massive IoT deployments, edge computing moving processing closer to devices, artificial intelligence enabling autonomous IoT decision-making, and the emergence of ambient computing environments where sensors and actuators are embedded invisibly throughout physical spaces. Each advancement introduces new security challenges that current defensive frameworks may not adequately address. 5G network slicing creates virtual networks for IoT deployments but introduces new trust boundaries that must be secured. Edge computing distributes processing and data storage to physically accessible locations, expanding the attack surface beyond hardened cloud data centers. AI-powered IoT systems make autonomous decisions that may be manipulated through adversarial inputs. Post-quantum cryptography threatens the viability of current IoT encryption schemes, and the long deployment lifecycles of IoT devices mean that devices being manufactured today must be prepared for quantum threats that may emerge during their operational lifetime. This session examines these emerging challenges and develops forward-looking security strategies.
key_concepts: 5G IoT security: network slicing isolation, massive Machine-Type Communication (mMTC) for large-scale IoT, enhanced subscriber identity protection (SUPI/SUCI), and new trust boundaries between network slice tenants

Edge computing security: distributed processing in physically accessible locations, container and VM isolation for multi-tenant edge, secure enclaves (SGX, TrustZone) for edge workload protection, and edge-to-cloud trust establishment

AI/ML for IoT security: anomaly detection using machine learning for fleet behavioral monitoring, adversarial attacks against AI-powered IoT systems, model poisoning through compromised training data, and AI-assisted threat detection for IoT networks

Post-quantum IoT cryptography: migration planning from RSA/ECC to lattice-based algorithms (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium), constraints of post-quantum algorithms on IoT devices with limited memory and processing power, hybrid approaches during transition

Ambient computing privacy: pervasive environmental sensors collecting audio, video, location, and behavioral data, data minimization and purpose limitation in smart environments, user consent challenges when sensors are invisible
Digital twin security: cloud-based virtual replicas of physical IoT systems used for monitoring and simulation, security implications of digital twin access, and integrity of twin synchronization

Crypto-agility for IoT: designing devices that can transition between cryptographic algorithms via firmware update, ensuring long-lived IoT devices can adopt new algorithms as current ones are deprecated or broken

Regulatory evolution: emerging IoT security legislation (EU Cyber Resilience Act, US IoT Cybersecurity Improvement Act), mandatory security labels for consumer IoT, and the impact of regulation on device design and lifecycle support
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating the post-quantum readiness of an IoT device's cryptographic implementation, identifying algorithms that will need replacement, assessing whether the device hardware supports the computational requirements of post-quantum algorithms, and developing a crypto-agility migration plan
setup_requirements: IoT device with configurable cryptographic libraries
Post-quantum cryptography reference implementations (liboqs)
Performance benchmarking tools for constrained devices
5G network simulation environment (optional)

review.value: 5G enables dedicated virtual network slices for IoT deployments with guaranteed performance and isolation. However, slice isolation must be verified: if an attacker can break slice boundaries, they can reach devices in other slices. Slice security verification is an emerging assessment discipline.

Many IoT devices deployed today will still be operational when quantum computers can break RSA and ECC. These devices need crypto-agility: the ability to update cryptographic algorithms via firmware update. Devices without update capability will become permanently vulnerable.

Smart buildings with microphones, cameras, and occupancy sensors create pervasive monitoring environments. Even anonymized data can be re-identified through behavioral patterns. Data minimization, local processing without cloud transmission, and clear privacy policies are essential ethical requirements.

The EU Cyber Resilience Act will require manufacturers to ensure IoT devices meet cybersecurity requirements throughout their lifecycle, including mandatory vulnerability handling and security updates. This shifts liability to manufacturers and will fundamentally change IoT product design and support commitments.

quizzes.topic: Mobile Security Architecture and IoT Attack Surface

questions.question: An Android application stores the user's OAuth token in SharedPreferences without encryption. On a rooted device, an attacker runs 'cat /data/data/com.example.app/shared_prefs/auth.xml' and obtains the token. Explain the vulnerability, the risk it creates, and two specific remediation approaches with technical details.
